title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
author: Jon Speed
orientation: Jon Speed responds to Toby Sumpter's claim that King Josiah was a 'smashmouth incrementalist' who exiled (rather than executed) idolatrous priests, arguing from Hebrew lexicons, the Septuagint, the Syriac Peshitta, and the wider commentary tradition that Josiah did in fact execute them. The article uses this exegetical case to indict pro-life incrementalism and defend the criminalization of abortion as a matter of biblical justice.
audiences: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
Pro-life activists weighing incremental legislation versus immediate abolition
Reformed and theonomic readers engaged with Cross Politic, Doug Wilson, and Toby Sumpter
Abolitionist legislators and citizens advocating equal-protection bills that criminalize abortion
Skeptics or critics who suspect abolitionists of being naive or uncharitable toward pro-life allies

questions.question: What is 'smashmouth incrementalism' and where did the term come from?
questions.audience: Reformed and theonomic readers engaged with Cross Politic, Doug Wilson, and Toby Sumpter
questions.answer: The term was coined by Doug Wilson in an October 2017 Blog & Mablog post and expanded by Toby Sumpter days later. Speed offers a working definition: aggressive and confrontational changes toward ending abortion pursued through incremental legislative steps.
questions.quote: The term "smash mouth incrementalism" was coined by Pastor Doug in a blog article called "Smashmouth Incrementalism" where it was not so much defined as described (*Blog & Mablog*, Tuesday October 10, 2017).  A few days later Toby expanded on Doug's blog post with one of his own

questions.question: What was Toby Sumpter's specific claim about King Josiah that triggered this article?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: On Cross Politic, Sumpter argued that Josiah did not execute the idolatrous priests but more likely removed them from Judah, making him a model 'smashmouth incrementalist' who was still recorded as a godly king and therefore a precedent for modern incremental abortion bills.
questions.quote: He cited King Josiah's actions from 2 Kings as an example of godly incrementalism. He claimed that Josiah did not execute the priests but rather was more likely to have removed them from Judah. Therefore, reasoned Toby, Josiah was a smashmouth incrementalist and was still recorded as a godly king.

questions.question: How does the Hebrew word shabat in 2 Kings 23:5 actually function, and does it support 'exile' as a translation?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: Speed shows that shabat in the Hiphil typically means 'to put an end to, to cause to cease,' and is a favorite prophetic verb for God's judgment. While 'remove' is a rare possibility, the immediate context of Josiah's covenantal zeal for the whole Law of Moses makes it an unlikely reading.
questions.quote: Victor P. Hamilton, the author of the article for *shabat* in TWOT, says that in the Hiphil, "…the meaning is 'to put an end to, to cause to cease'.  Preponderantly, in the Hiphil of *shabat*, God is the subject of the verb.  It is a favorite verb of the prophets to describe God's judgment on His people (Is. 13:11)"

questions.question: What does the Septuagint suggest Josiah did to the idolatrous priests?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: Speed's pastor, a trained Greek scholar, parses the Septuagint verb in 2 Kings 23:5 as an aorist active indicative meaning 'burn down, burn up, consume by fire,' rendering the line, 'And he burned up the pagan priest.' This aligns with the Hebrew burning language used of Jehoshaphat in 1 Kings 22:46.
questions.quote: He says, "The Greek word is in the Aorist Active Indicative, 3rd Singular and is used to describe the whole event that happened.  The word's definition is 'burn down, burn up, consume by fire' (Danker.  A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature.  517)". In a private message Pastor Easter said that he would translate the first line of the Septuagint's version of the verse, "And he burned up the pagan priest"

questions.question: What does the Syriac Peshitta tradition contribute to the interpretation?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: Multiple Semitic versions render the verb as 'he slew' or 'they killed.' Because the Peshitta is one of the oldest dated Bible manuscripts in any language, its agreement with execution carries significant evidential weight for the meaning of 2 Kings 23:5.
questions.quote: In the Syriac Peshitta Bible with English Translation, the verse is translated, "***They killed*** the pagan priests whom the kings of Judah had established to burn incense on the high places…"

questions.question: Did any Bible scholar Speed consulted teach that the priests of 2 Kings 23:5 emigrated rather than were executed?
questions.audience: Skeptics or critics who suspect abolitionists of being naive or uncharitable toward pro-life allies
questions.answer: After consulting roughly fifty volumes at Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary plus his own library, Speed found that not a single scholar in those collections suggested the idolatrous priests or male cult prostitutes emigrated under Josiah's direction.
questions.quote: However, the big takeaway from my study of these many commentaries, Bible dictionary articles and monographs is this: ***not ONE Bible scholar represented in these volumes suggested that the idolatrous priests of 2 Kings 23:5 or the male cult prostitutes of 2 Kings 23:7 emigrated out of the land under Josiah's direction.***  Not one.

questions.question: Why does Speed think nineteenth-century commentators softened Josiah's zeal, and what's wrong with that?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: Many older interpreters were embarrassed by Josiah and read New Covenant grace back into his reforms, treating Deuteronomy's commands as merely figurative. Speed sees this as an anachronism and warns that Sumpter is repeating the same move in his pastoral reluctance to criminalize abortion-murder.
questions.quote:
May not Deut. xiii. 6-18 have been intended to express in a concrete but ideal form the spirit of execration to be entertained toward idolatry?  Perhaps in thinking so we are only guilty of an anachronism, and are applying to the seventh century before Christ the feelings of the nineteenth century after Christ" (391-92). 

I suspect that Pastor Toby is guilty of the same anachronism in his pastoral desire to withhold criminal penalty from those who commit abortion-murder.

questions.question: Why does Speed reject the 'we're all on the same team' framing of the pro-life movement?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists weighing incremental legislation versus immediate abolition
questions.answer: He argues the secular, Roman Catholic-led pro-life movement consistently runs counterproductive plays while keeping abolitionists benched, and that pro-life lobbyists and politicians collude with the system to keep abortion legal. He calls this rigged game and urges a #PLEXIT.
questions.quote: While it appears that abolitionists and pro-lifers are on the same team, the game is fixed.  It's rigged. Do not bet against Roe v. Wade or the SCOTUS' desire to uphold that wicked decree.   It's not likely that anyone in Vegas is betting against the pro-choice\pro-life collusion to keep abortion legal.  At some point we need to leave the secular, Roman Catholic-led pro-life establishment with its doctrine of Balaam and we need to field a team that actually has a plan to win the game.  It is high time for a #PLEXIT (pro-life exit).

questions.question: How does Speed answer the pastoral objection that criminalizing abortion is unmerciful to post-abortive women?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: He calls the objection a category error: the church ministers grace, but the government, per Romans 13, ministers justice and bears the sword against evildoers. The standard for civil good and evil is the Law of God, which prescribes the death penalty for causing the death of a child in the womb.
questions.quote: When we as pastors suggest that we should legislate with grace toward the abortive parents they commit a logical fallacy–a category error. The church ministers grace.  But the government ministers justice. Romans 13 is clear: it bears the sword against the evil doer.

questions.question: What does Speed see as the cumulative lesson from Asa, Jehoshaphat, and Josiah for today's strategy?
questions.audience: Abolitionist legislators and citizens advocating equal-protection bills that criminalize abortion
questions.answer: Even Josiah's valiant abolitionism could not undo generations of incremental compromise; Judah still went into Babylonian captivity. Read individually, Josiah models abolition, but the dynastic pattern warns that incrementalism postpones reform until it is too late.
questions.quote: Additionally, the cumulative effect of the reigns of all of the kings of Judah, in spite of Josiah's valiant efforts, was total judgment in the form of Babylonian captivity.  Josiah's abolitionism was not enough to overcome the generations of incrementalism and compromise.

questions.question: How does Speed use Augustine to frame the duty of Christian magistrates?
questions.audience: Reformed and theonomic readers engaged with Cross Politic, Doug Wilson, and Toby Sumpter
questions.answer: Speed closes by quoting Augustine's distinction that a sovereign serves God as a man by personal faith, but as a king by enacting laws that command goodness and prohibit its opposite. Magistrates therefore have a uniquely kingly service of God that pastors should call them to.
questions.quote: A sovereign serves God one way as man, another way as king; he serves Him as man by living according to faith, he serves Him as king by exerting the necessary strength to sanction laws that command goodness and prohibit its opposite…It is thus that kings serve the Lord as kings when they perform acts in His service that none but kings can perform

questions.question: What is the controlling hermeneutical rule Speed invokes against Sumpter's reading?
questions.audience: Pastors and Bible teachers wrestling with how Old Testament law applies to civil magistrates today
questions.answer: Speed appeals to a basic interpretive principle that an interpretation no other scholar verifies is, by that fact, suspect. Granting Sumpter respect, he still concludes the 'exile' reading of 2 Kings 23:5 is exegesis read into the text rather than out of it.
questions.quote: It is a basic rule of hermeneutics that if you come up with an interpretation that cannot be verified by other scholars that you have a misinterpretation of the text.  With all due respect to Pastor Toby–and he is due MUCH more respect than I have space for here–his interpretation is a misinterpretation.

related.title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
related.reason: James Silberman's direct, sustained rebuttal of Sumpter's published doctrine of smashmouth incrementalism, the same school of thought Speed engages here.

related.title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
related.reason: Targets the Moscow, ID Cross Politic / Doug Wilson camp that originated the smashmouth incrementalism category Speed critiques.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Published the same week as Speed's piece and gives the practical legislative case against the heartbeat-style bills Speed dismisses as incrementalism ad nauseum.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: A concise framing of the immediatism/incrementalism debate that Speed presupposes throughout the article.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the theological argument against incrementalism from the holiness of God, complementing Speed's argument from Josiah's covenantal zeal for the Law.

related.title: Don't Fall For Texas Right to Life's Faux Abolitionism
related.reason: Documents the 'pro-life referees' Speed names (Texas Right to Life, Texas Alliance for Life) who keep abolition bills off the field.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Lays out the abolitionist position on equal protection / criminalization of abortion that Speed defends as the biblical-justice alternative to pastoral grace-as-law.
